div.head: 381. Memorandum From the Assistant Secretary of State for African Affairs (Williams) to Secretary of State RuskSource: Department of State, Central Files, 750G.00/12– 2162. Top Secret. Drafted by Godley.
div.p: We have just received the following message which Ambassador Gullion sent via CIA channels after he was informed that Parliament had unanimously requested the release of Mr. Gizenga.

“Although fully aware of proprieties in so delicate a matter, would appreciate any kind of guidance or indication of tolerable margins which USG can give me with respect to influencing GOC crisis now that Gizenga release has been directed by unanimous vote Chamber.

“If Govt stands by its guns and disobeys order; showdown can be expected. I do not believe, however, that Govt will stand firm without encouragement from outside which I am inclined to give unless dissuaded. I have never said nor will I say now more than that my Govt would not change its attitude toward GOC if it felt obliged to take such measures. I have also informed GOC that almost all diplomatic missions Leo would favor GOC assertion of authority. Preferably this would take form immediate proroguing Parliament minimum 30 days including Christmas holiday period. This might be accompanied by showdown on austerity laws, demand for constituent assembly, declaration of program with respect to Katanga. Essential fact is that if we continue on this course Leftist coalition will come into power even though right-wing Belgian-backed element may appear momentarily as facade.
“We are heading for return of Lumumba era.
“Presence Truman mission at this time may turn out to be source of embarrassment. At same time may be able to use it to stiffen backbone Kasavubu whom I am seeing at 1300 today.”
We believe we should inform Ambassador Gullion to proceed in an effort to influence men around Adoula to permit Adoula to rule by extra-Parliamentary means.

Although such action will forestall Gizenga’s release and increase Adoula’s liberty of action, we should have no illusion that it would bring about a more conciliatory immediate attitude by Léopoldville authorities in negotiating with Tshombe. While moderate leadership
will have been assured and over the long run it will be more amenable to Western influences, we will also have created a situation where we will be wedded to those whom we have supported.
This support may well become more onerous in the future. We undoubtedly will be able to continue to guide the government but at the same time we anticipate the government may be more demanding on us.

Finally, I might add that if we approve the Ambassador moving forward on this matter the CIA would proceed to implement the covert annexNot found. to the National Security Council paper, that was approved over this last weekend.

Notwithstanding the drawbacks to extra-legal action, I believe that Ambassador Gullion should proceed at this time to endeavor to forestall the fall of the moderate government in Léopoldville.No reply to Gullion’s message has been found.
